title: Adhesion in EPDM joints: Role of the interdiffusion mechanism on interfacial co-crosslinking
abstract:
Adhesion in EPDM Joints: Role of the Interdiffusion
Mechanism on Interfacial Co-crosslinking
F. RUCH, M. O. DAVID, M. F. VALLAT
Institut de Chimie des Surfaces et Interfaces, UPR 9069 – CNRS, 15, rue Jean Starcky, B.P. 2488,
Mulhouse, Cedex 68057, France
Received 16 December 1999; revised 26 July 2000; accepted 12 September 2000
ABSTRACT: The purpose of this study was to understand the relationship between the
mechanism of interdiffusion of the polymer chains across the interface and the forma-
tion of crosslinks in the interfacial zone when two elastomer sheets are joined and
crosslinked. It is commonly accepted that the strength of the interface thus obtained is
related to the number of interlinks that are created in the molecular interphase. This
number generally is considered as equal to the number of crosslinks determined in the
bulk. Ethylene-copropylene-codiene polymer (EPDM) does not follow this general law.
The slow diffusion of the chains at the interface may be responsible for the peculiar
behavior observed. In order to separate the two mechanisms responsible for the inter-
facial strength, diffusion, and crosslinking, two crosslinking procedures, namely per-
oxide crosslinking at high temperature and electron beam crosslinking at room tem-
perature, have been used. This latter procedure allows control of the diffusion depth. It
has been shown that diffusion of EPDM chains is indeed occurring at a much slower
rate than expected, leading to less efﬁcient co-crosslinking in the interfacial zone. © 2000
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci B: Polym Phys 38: 3189–3199, 2000
Keywords:
adhesion; strength; interlinking; elastomers interfacial bonding
INTRODUCTION
The formation of an elastomer-to-elastomer inter-
face can be separated into three different steps
(Fig. 1):
●“wetting” of the surfaces leading to an inti-
mate contact;
●diffusion across the interface of free or dan-
gling chains; and
●covalent bonding of diffused chains across
the 
interface 
(co-crosslinking) 
when 
a
crosslinking procedure is added.
Self-adhesion, also called autohesion, of elas-
tomers is a time-dependent behavior. This behav-
ior was ﬁrst studied by Voyutskii1 to support dif-
fusion mechanisms of chains across the interface.
After a while, chain entanglements (after a min-
imum interpenetration depth2) and homogeniza-
tion occur, at least in noncrosslinked systems.
Due to the high molecular weight of the polymers,
the reptation theory of de Gennes applies.3 How-
ever, the same behavior can be used to support
the wetting mechanism. Indeed it has been shown
that viscous ﬂow, which depends on the applied
pressure, increases the contact area between non-
perfectly smooth elastomer layers. At this point,
there still is no consensus on the predominant
aspect and the exact role of interdiffusion is un-
decided.4 However Wool’s5 theory of time-depen-
dent wetting followed by interdiffusion is a rea-
sonable assumption.
In the case of autohesion, the diffusion of the
chains through the interface between two identi-
Correspondence
to:
M.-F.
Vallat
(E-mail:
MF.Vallat@
univ-mulhouse.fr)
Journal of Polymer Science: Part B: Polymer Physics, Vol. 38, 3189–3199 (2000)
© 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
3189
 cal polymer sheets cannot be followed unless
chemical marking (mainly deuteration) has been
done on the polymer chains on one side. Only
indirect analysis is possible, mainly by measuring
the interfacial strength of the assemblies in a
destructive test. The experimental conditions of
separation may allow one to distinguish the
debonding mechanisms: interdiffused chains are
broken when the separation occurs at high rates
or low temperatures (the energy of separation is
strongly dependent on the separation rate) and
are extracted at lower rates (the energy of sepa-
ration becomes independent on the separation
rate).
Crosslinking of the joined polymers leads to the
formation of a three-dimensional network in the
bulk material. When chains have crossed the in-
terface, they can be covalently bonded to the op-
posite network on both sides. In order to distin-
guish the chains crosslinked at the interface and
in the bulk, the term co-crosslinking will be used
although the same chemical bonds are created at
the interface and in the bulk (distinguished by
stars and dots in Fig. 1).
Gent et al.6,7 have shown that when two elas-
tomer sheets are crosslinked in contact, the me-
chanical strength of a joint under near equilib-
rium conditions (or threshold strength) is directly
proportional to the amount of chemical interlink-
ing, as evaluated from measurements of the bulk
density of crosslinking.
The exact contribution of the interdiffusion and
interlinking mechanisms is quite difﬁcult to de-
termine because, in general, crosslinking is per-
formed at a high temperature, above 120 °C. At
these temperatures, the diffusion of the chains is
very rapid and complete healing of the interface
may be expected before the chains are immobi-
lized by the crosslinks.
The relationship between threshold strength
and the density of interfacial crosslinks, Dn ap-
pears as a general law. However, the interfacial
autohesion strength for EPDM (ethylene-copro-
pylene-codiene terpolymer) does not follow the
same rule, as shown by Chang and Gent,8 al-
though ethylene propylene copolymer (EPR) does.
This difference in behavior cannot be due to the
lower crosslinking efﬁciency of EPDM; the two
polymers differ only by the introduction in the
polymer chain backbone of a third monomer of
diene type, which helps to crosslink the system
more easily. Two main assumptions have been
considered by Chang and Gent8 in order to ex-
plain the anomalous behavior of EPDM: (1) the
erroneous evaluation of the amount of interfacial
bonding, deduced from bulk density of crosslinks,
and (2) the effect of the molding substrate and,
especially, of migration of the accelerator of the
sulfur curing system into the Mylart substrate.
The use of a different experiment method to
determine the number of bonds created leads to
the same non-linear relationship. Replacement of
the Mylart ﬁlm by ferro-type molding substrate
changes the results slightly but does not modify
the non-linear dependence of G on Dn. We have
concluded that this anomalous behavior requires
Figure 1.
Formation of the interface between two
elastomer sheets. (a) wetting stage (b) interdiffusion
stage (c) co-crosslinking stage (dots: crosslinks, stars:
co-crosslinks)
3190
RUCH, DAVID, AND VALLAT

and the density of interfacial crosslinks, Dn ap-
pears as a general law. However, the interfacial

pylene-codiene terpolymer) does not follow the
same rule, as shown by Chang and Gent,8 al-
though ethylene propylene copolymer (EPR) does.
This difference in behavior cannot be due to the

polymers differ only by the introduction in the
polymer chain backbone of a third monomer of
diene type, which helps to crosslink the system
more easily. Two main assumptions have been
considered by Chang and Gent8 in order to ex-
plain the anomalous behavior of EPDM: (1) the

bonding, deduced from bulk density of crosslinks,
and (2) the effect of the molding substrate and,
especially, of migration of the accelerator of the
sulfur curing system into the Mylart substrate.

determine the number of bonds created leads to

the Mylart ﬁlm by ferro-type molding substrate
changes the results slightly but does not modify

Formation of the interface between two
elastomer sheets. (a) wetting stage (b) interdiffusion
stage (c) co-crosslinking stage (dots: crosslinks, stars:
further investigation. It is reasonable to note at
this point that the number of interlinks is never
measured directly. It always is deduced from bulk
measurements, assuming that it is identical in
the bulk and in the interfacial zone. In the
present work we will show that the number of
interfacial bonds depends on the size of the inter-
facial domain formed by the interdiffusion of the
chains. In other words, when the penetration
length of the chains at the interface is limited, it
will be reﬂected in the crosslinking efﬁciency in
What can slow down the diffusion rate of the
EPDM chains compared to the EPR chains? Both
EPR and EPDM show a high degree of entangle-
ment, as indicated by the low value of the molec-
ular weight between entanglements, Me ('1700–
1900 g/mol).9,10 The polymerization of the terpoly-
mer introduces branching in the structure, which
affects the diffusion coefﬁcients as well as the
chain connectivity.11 Whatever the reason for the
limitation of the interdiffusion mechanism, we
are interested here in the inﬂuence of that mech-
anism on the interfacial properties in order to
explain the decrease in the number of interlinks
and, consequently, the anomalous relationship
between the interfacial strength and the degree of
For that purpose, the degree of interlinking
was varied by using a two-step procedure for
preparation of the samples. In order to test our
assumption, two different crosslinking proce-
dures leading to C-C bonds were used. The classic
procedure of crosslinking by peroxide, where the
diffusion of the chains at the interface and the
network formation occur simultaneously, was
performed at a high temperature. Then curing by
electron beam at room temperature was used to
separate the effect of the covalent bonds between
the chains from the effect of the diffusion of the
chains across the interface. Indeed, in the absence
of a curing agent, the diffusion of the chains can
be done at various temperatures and lengths of
time, rendering it possible to control the diffusion
depth independent of the network formation.
The chemical composition of the elastomer EPDM
(Buna AP 541) used and kindly supplied by
Bayer, is shown in Table I. Ethylidene nor-
bornene (ENB) is the termonomer. The glass
transition of the polymer is around 255 °C, and
the Mooney viscosity ML (1 1 4) at 125 °C is equal
The network characterization was done by swell-
ing measurements at equilibrium in cyclohexane
and application of the Flory–Rehner relationship.
The sol fraction, fs, was measured, and the den-
sity of efﬁcient chains, nc, was calculated accord-
where vs is the molar volume of the swelling sol-
vent, VR is the volume fraction of polymer in the
swollen sample at equilibrium, and x is the poly-
mer/solvent interaction parameter (equal to 0.321
crosslinking can be reached by varying the
amount of peroxide introduced, by partial decom-
position of the peroxide (accomplished by chang-
ing the curing time), or by electron beam curing.
EPDM was mixed with dicumyl peroxide (DCuP;
1 or 2.5 parts) on an open-roll mill at 50 °C. One
part of an antioxidant, IPPD (N-isopropyl-N’-phe-
nyl-p-phenylenediamine), was added before the
peroxide. The mix then was compression-molded
into sheets approximately 1-mm thick between
clean Mylart ﬁlms at a temperature of 150 °C
under pressure for 150 min in an electrically
heated press. The evolution of the density of efﬁ-
cient chains (nc) and sol fraction (fs) for EPDM
containing 2.5 phr of DCuP is shown as a function
The samples were irradiated in air by an electron
beam accelerator (Model AS2000 from HVEC) at
AERIAL in Strasbourg (France).13 This accelera-
tor operates at 2.2 MeV. The samples were placed
in a conveyor whose speed can be adjusted from 1
3 1023 to 8 3 1022 m/s. The speed of the conveyor
and the number of crossings through the beam
deﬁne the dose received by the sample. It varied
from 20 to 260 kGy. For technical reasons and in
order to limit the heating of the samples (a few
degrees every 10 kGy), a maximum dose of 50 kGy
per pass was administered. Figure 3 presents the
network characteristics as a function of the cur-
ing dose for EPDM free of peroxide but containing
1 phr of IPPD. A dose of about 200 kGy leads to an
efﬁcient chain density of 70 mol/m3 and a sol
fraction of about 13%. It has been checked that
the crosslinking efﬁciency is not dependent on the
thickness of the samples in the thickness range
A three-step procedure was used. During the
ﬁrst step, called the pre-curing step, the two elas-
tomer sheets were crosslinked separately to the
same level characterized by n1 and fs. The second
step is called the contact step: the two elastomer
sheets are brought into intimate contact under
pressure. The time (tc) and temperature (Tc) of
the contact were varied over domains in which
the degree of crosslinking of the elastomer did
not change. The third step during which the
crosslinking in the bulk and eventually in the
interfacial region occurs. The bulk networks in
the ﬁnal state are characterized by n and fs. The
pre-curing step always was done with peroxide
while the post-curing corresponded either to the
decomposition of the residual peroxide in the
sample or to the electron-beam curing. The as-
semblies always were compared at the same ﬁnal
level of crosslinking in the bulk so that the bulk
dissipation during the interfacial strength evalu-
Interfacial Strength Evaluation
The interfacial strength of elastomer joints com-
monly is evaluated by a peel test. We used a
T-peel test geometry with a backing added to the
elastomer sheets before curing to avoid extension
during peeling. The fracture energy, G, is shown
where F is the measured force and v the width of
The results shown here were obtained at room
temperature and with various peel rates, R, rang-
The various conditions used for the joint prepara-
tion have been chosen in order to show the con-
tribution of the two mechanisms. They are sum-
Density of efﬁcient chains and sol fraction
Density of efﬁcient chains and sol fraction
as a function of curing time at 150 °C for EPDM con-
Influence of the Pre-cure Time
Two elastomer sheets containing 2.5 phr of DCuP
were cured separately at 150 °C for various cure
times (t1). The joint formation is described in Fig-
ure 4(a). For times shorter than the decomposi-
tion time of the peroxide (about 100 min at 150
°C), only part of the peroxide reacted and the
sheets were partially crosslinked to a degree, n1.
Then the sheets were put into contact and further
cured for t2 up to 150 min (t1 1 t2 5 150), leading
to a bulk crosslinking degree of n. In this last step,
high diffusion and co-crosslinking effects are ex-
pected because of the high temperature. The ﬁnal
bulk material properties then were identical
whatever the degree of pre-cure, and the fracture
energies could be compared. The results plotted
on Figure 5 were obtained for a peel rate of 5
mm/min. When the pre-cure time became shorter,
the peel strength was higher. When two fully
cured sheets were joined (t1 5 150 min), the peel
strength was low (20 J.m22); however, it still was
higher than the reversible work of adhesion,
about 0.05 J.m22, showing that energy dissipa-
tion is not negligible. For pre-cure times equal to
or less than 20 min, the measured strength was
roughly equal to the tear strength at the same
rate. The failure, even in this case, propagated
smoothly at the interface, showing that it remains
Procedures used for joint formation and expected inﬂuences.
Variation of peel strength with pre-curing
time at a peel rate of 5 mm/min.
the weak point of the assemblies. The same ob-
servations were reported by Chang and Gent8 for
EPR and Roland and Bo¨hm,14 who studied auto-
hesion of polybutadienes. This evolution can be
explained by the decrease of the quantity of per-
oxide available for co-crosslinking when the pre-
cure time becomes longer. Moreover, the amount
of free chains also decreases as the degree of
pre-crosslinking increases. However, it is gener-
ally not true for reinforced rubber for which the
peel test becomes impossible as soon as the num-
ber of interfacial crosslinks rises.15
The fracture energy can be related to the de-
gree of interlinking (Dn), as proposed by Gent.
where n and n1, respectively, are equal to the
density of efﬁcient network chains after complete
curing and partial curing during t1 min at 150 °C.
Peel strength versus Dn is plotted in Figure 6.
Our results are compared to the results of the
threshold peel strength (when the dissipated en-
ergy is negligible in the swollen state and at low
peel rate) published by Chang and Gent.8 Peel
strengths cannot be directly compared because
the networks are not identical and the conditions
of peeling are different (in our case, R 5 5 mm/
min). However, it can be observed that the shapes
of the curves are comparable and that no linear
relationship can be proposed between G and Dn,
contrary to what is generally found.6–8 The num-
ber of interfacial crosslinks appears to have been
overestimated when compared with the expected
linear relationship, as shown in Figure 6. This
demonstrates that crosslinking is less efﬁcient at
the interface than in the bulk. The assumption
made by Chang and Gent,8 based on contamina-
tion by an insoluble by-product of the sulfur-cur-
ing system preventing intimate contact, has been
suggested. No such contaminant is expected with
our samples as they are crosslinked by peroxide.
An insufﬁcient diffusion across the interface
could be another explanation of this anomalous
behavior. Crosslinking occurs at a high tempera-
ture with peroxide so that the chains are immo-
bilized before they have time to sufﬁciently cross
the interface. With this crosslinking procedure it
is difﬁcult to distinguish chemical bonding from
diffusion. We therefore made assemblies without
a post-cure step in order to get the sole contribu-
tion of the diffused chains to the interfacial
Two cases were considered. In the ﬁrst case, net-
works with a high number of free chains were
yielded by a partial decomposition of 2.5 phr of
DCuP mixed with EPDM. In the second case, fully
cured samples led to networks with a low concen-
tration of free chains [Fig. 4(b)].
EPDM sheets containing 2.5 phr of DCuP were
heated for 20 min at 150 °C between Mylart ﬁlms
and then submerged in ice water to stop the
crosslinking reaction. The density of the efﬁcient
network chains and the sol fraction were approx-
imately 2–4 mol/m3 and 45%, respectively. Two
sheets then were brought into contact after peel-
off of the protective substrate. The contact step
varied from 5 min to 2900 min at room tempera-
ture. Peel strength is plotted as a function of peel
rate in Figure 7. The results obtained with a
contact step at a higher temperature (95 °C) for
1 h are shown on the same plot. At both temper-
atures of contact, no further crosslinking can oc-
cur because the peroxide decomposition is negli-
gible. Although the results are quite scattered, a
straight line can be plotted with a slope equal to
about 0.5. This would be characteristic of joint
failure with chain extraction due to a diffusion
mechanism of free or dangling chains, as shown
by Wool.16 No signiﬁcant effect of the time of
Peel strength versus the degree of inter-
linking of the two sheets crosslinked into contact (our
results: peel rate 5 5 mm/min; Chang and Gent’s re-
sults: threshold peel strength). The straight line is
drawn to show the expected law.
contact was observed between 5 and 850 min
whereas an important increase was observed for
2900 min. A contact of 1 h at 95 °C led to the same
peel strength. The ﬁlled-in points in Figure 7
correspond to the tear strength. Therefore, the
cohesive strength of the material at the same
level of crosslinking is not reached for the consid-
ered contact conditions, showing that diffusion is
very slow. It should be noted that for peel rates
higher than 5 mm/min, a stick-slip type of sepa-
ration generally occurs due to the low degree of
crosslinking of the elastomer. We should point out
that the interfacial strength of these non-post-
cured samples is quite difﬁcult to evaluate due to
their low modulus and high extensibility. The
increase of interfacial strength after a long time
of contact or at higher temperatures clearly shows
that diffusion of chains is occurring although it
seems to be a very slow process.
crosslinked for 150 min at 150 °C, leading to
networks with nc equal to 120 mol/m3 and a sol
fraction of 5%. All the peroxide introduced was
decomposed, and we can therefore consider the
effect of the temperature of contact without fear
strength versus peel rate are shown in Figure 8.
It can be seen that no signiﬁcant effect of the
temperature of contact for 1 or 2 h appears. As
before, the results can be plotted on a slope close
to 0.5. For values higher than 50 mm/min, the
peel strength becomes less dependent on the peel
rate, which indicates that the mechanism of frac-
ture of the joints is different. Since temperature of
contact has no effect (or a slight one if one con-
siders the behavior at 150 °C for 2 h), it is most
likely that the chain diffusion is almost complete
after 1 h at room temperature and principally
applies to chains that are very mobile. According
to Kausch,17 healing of crosslinked thermoplas-
tics occurs through chain ends (very rapid but less
effective) and deformed chains that contain no or
just one or two crosslinking points. The free
chains remaining in the network considered here
were short, as was observed by GPC analysis.
Figure 9 shows that the molecular weight of the
chains in the sol fraction decrease when the du-
ration of crosslinking increases. They do not con-
tribute signiﬁcantly to the transmission of me-
chanical stress. Diffusion of dangling chains also
may play a role if they are close to the interface,
as does hair-pin-type diffusion with respect to the
low degree of crosslinking of the elastomer (Mc
GPC results of the free chains as a function
of curing time at 150 °C for an EPDM crosslinked with
Peel strength versus peel rate for various
contact times at room temperature and no post-cure for
lightly crosslinked samples (nc 5 2–4 mol/m3 and fs 5
Peel strength versus peel rate for various
temperatures of contact for post-crosslinked samples
(nc 5 120 mol/m3 and fs 5 5%).
. 7000 g/mol). However, the measured peel
strength is very low and therefore demonstrates
that these chains are not very effective as long as
they are not chemically bonded.
These two experiments show that diffusion
phenomena are partly responsible for interfacial
strength. However, very long periods of times are
necessary to enhance interfacial strength due to
the slow diffusion of the longest chains. Roland
and Bo¨hm14 found that the self-diffusion coefﬁ-
cient determined directly does not always lead to
a good estimate of the macromolecular self-diffu-
sion as inferred from strength measurements.
This discrepancy was shown for polybutadiene
joints and even more strongly for autohesion of
ethylene-propylene copolymer. Extrapolation of
the results given would indicate that about 140
days at room temperature would be necessary to
reach full strength. The presence of branched ma-
terial was considered by the authors to be respon-
sible for the observed behavior. Any measurable
crack rehealing was observed by Nguyen et al.18
even after several days of penetration above Tg
when the samples of styrene-acrylonitrile copoly-
mer were slightly crosslinked before rehealing.
At this point, the question becomes: Is the
strength of peroxide post-cured joints inﬂuenced
by the interdiffusion state reached after the con-
tact step? Crosslinking points are created in the
bulk of the elastomer and perhaps in the interfa-
Influence of Chain Diffusion and Co-crosslinking
The EPDM sheets containing 2.5 phr of DCuP
were partially crosslinked for 20 min at 150 °C (n
5 2–4 mol/m3 and fs 5 40–50%). Residual perox-
ide was still active in the elastomer and free
chains were present in great quantity. The sheets
then were joined for various lengths of time at 95
°C before the crosslinking step at 150 °C for 130
min. At 95 °C, only chain diffusion occurs whereas
at 150 °C crosslinking and chain diffusion are
possible. The results shown in Figure 10 indicate
that the contact step has no effect on the interfa-
cial strength of peroxide post-crosslinked sam-
ples. The peel strength is close to the tear
strength in the whole range of peel rates consid-
ered. Therefore, chains diffuse enough before the
crosslinking efﬁciency is the same in the interfa-
cial domain whatever the degree of penetration
reached after the contact step. It is not possible to
crosslinking. A post-cure at ambient temperature,
by electron beam, must be used.
Separation of the Contributions of Chain Diffusion
and the Interfacial Crosslinking–Post-curing by
Assemblies of Uncrosslinked EPDM
The elastomer was mixed with 1 phr of IPPD and
molded between Mylart ﬁlms to produce smooth
sheets. The EPDM sheets were joined for various
times between 1 and 200 h at room temperature
or for 2 h at 150 °C [Fig. 4(c)]. No peroxide was
present and therefore only chain diffusion was
expected. After the contact step, the joints were
irradiated with a dose of 150 kGy. The density of
the elastic chains was about 55 mol/m3 and the sol
fraction was 15%. As in the previous experiment,
the joints were peeled at various rates (0.05 mm/
min up to 50 mm/min) at room temperature. The
results are reported in Figure 11. The peel
strengths cannot be compared quantitatively
with those of Figure 8 because the degree of
crosslinking of the networks is not identical, and
therefore the energy dissipated during separation
is different. Nevertheless, peel strength increases
with peel rate, as expected. However, it can be
observed that the slope of the straight line (R
, 10 mm/min) is lower than 0.5. This indicates
that chain scission occurs during separation ei-
ther because of the chemical bridging of the
chains or because of chain entanglements. In-
deed, at a ﬁnite rate, the entangled chains behave
like chemically bonded chains.
However, the peel strength is not proportional to
the square root of the contact time and is rather a
complex function of it, as can be seen in Figure 12.
Peel strength versus peel rate for perox-
ide post-crosslinked samples—degree of pre-cure, n1
A ﬁrst increase is followed by a decrease for a longer
period of time and there is no possible separation for
a contact of 200 h. It also is very surprising to note
that a sample of two sheets of elastomer that were
in contact for 2 hours at 150 °C were peelable. For a
tear rate of 5 mm/min, tear strength is given as a
function of the degree of crosslinking in Figure 13.
For a sample with nc of about 55 mol/m3, the extrap-
olated value of tear strength (' 2000 J/m2) is less
than half the peel strength (' 5000 J/m2) measured
after a contact of 2 h at 150 °C. The energy dissi-
pated during the separation by peeling the joint is
higher. This can be due to a longer chain length of
interlinks in the interfacial domain, which would
not be homogenized at the molecular level because
of slow diffusion even at 150 °C. This explanation
also would hold for the behavior observed for a
contact of 200 h. Again, if the degree of interlinking
is smaller than the degree of crosslinking in the
bulk, the interfacial chains tend to elongate rather
than fail. There is, then, a question of interfacial
mechanical stress transfer. Chun and Gent19 have
shown that the loss function is greater when the
chains that are connected to the networks on both
sides of the interface are few and long. It is lower
when they are numerous and shorter.
Assemblies of Partially Crosslinked EPDM
Sheets of EPDM (100 phr of rubber 1 1 phr of DCuP
1 1 phr of IPPD) were crosslinked for 1 h at 160 °C.
Lightly crosslinked networks (n 5 10 mol/m3) were
obtained as a result. The sol fraction (fs) still corre-
sponded to a signiﬁcant amount of polymer (25%).
There was no peroxide left after this heat treat-
ment. Two series of experiments then were per-
formed. For the ﬁrst series, the joints were made by
contact of two sheets at room temperature during
lengths of time ranging from 1 h up to 624 h (1
month). For the second series, the contact was made
at various temperatures ranging from 25 up to 170
°C. After this contact, the samples were irradiated
with a constant dose of 130 kGy to produce net-
works with n 5 105 mol/m3 and fs 5 8%. The joint
formation is summarized in Figure 4(d).
Peel strength at 5 mm/min is plotted in Figure 14
as a function of the time of contact at room temper-
ature. It can be observed that up to approximately
500 h, there is no effect of the contact time on
interfacial strength. For longer times, there are
some trends for increased strength. These trends
were reinforced by another observation: when these
samples were immersed in a good solvent, such as
cyclohexane, there was no spontaneous separation
of the sheets, contrary to what was observed for
times shorter than 300 h. Nevertheless, even after 1
month of contact before crosslinking, the peel
strength of the joints is much weaker than the tear
strength of the same material (about 1/3).
Peel strength versus peel rate for irradi-
ated samples—no pre-cure step (nc 5 55 mol/m3 and fs
Evolution of the peel strength with con-
Tear strength as a function of molecular
weight between crosslinks for R 5 5 mm/min.
The second set of experiments was conducted by
changing the temperature of contact for a constant
time, 1 h. Figure 15 shows that interfacial strength
is directly related to the temperature of the contact
step. The peel strength increases gradually with the
temperature of contact. It also shows that the tear
strength of EPDM at the same level of crosslinking
and the same rate of crack propagation (R 5 5
mm/min) is reached only after a contact of 1 h at 170
°C. Time does not seem to play an important role, as
shown by the mean peel strengths obtained for
times ranging from 30 min up to 6 h at various
temperatures and reported in the same ﬁgure. The
high uncertainty was common to most of the sam-
ples with a low degree of crosslinking. If the process
is thermally activated, energetic barriers have to be
overcome. The activation energy calculated is equal
Although the respective effects of time and
temperature are not well understood and need
more work, this is, to our knowledge, the ﬁrst
time that the contributions of chain diffusion and
co-crosslinking to interfacial strength clearly
have been separated. Electron-beam curing would
seem to be a very promising technique to use for
This study of the interfacial strength of elastomer–
elastomer joints has conﬁrmed the peculiar behav-
ior of EPDM. The number of bonds in the interfacial
zone cannot be reached directly, and therefore the
adhesive behavior in a peel test has to be inter-
preted by indirect evaluation of the interlinks. The
nonlinear relationship between peel strength and
the number of bonds has been attributed to the very
slow and unexpected diffusion of the elastomer
chains due to the high degree of entanglements
combined with chain branching introduced during
the polymerization process. When crosslinking pro-
ceeds by high temperature reaction, it is not possi-
ble to control interdiffusion and co-crosslinking sep-
arately; both phenomena occur simultaneously and
are competitive. By using a crosslinking procedure
at room temperature, that is, by electron beam ir-
radiation, it has been shown that the efﬁciency of
the crosslinking reaction in the interfacial zone is
clearly related to the interpenetration depth al-
though the drastic difference in the inﬂuence of the
time and temperature of contact are not under-
